metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the convergence-viz oracle to assert the correct invariant: for a ≥4-round ticket, assert `<svg class="conv-line">` is present and `<polyline>` count equals the number of DISTINCT decision series present in the fixture data (not the round count), specifying in the oracle the exact series the fixture exercises (e.g., 'fixture has BLOCKING+advisory → assert exactly 2 `<polyline>` elements').
evidence: Plan (What section): 'an `<svg class="conv-line">` holding one `<polyline>` per decision series (BLOCKING prominent, advisory/dropped/indeterminate muted)'
AC: 'a 4-round ticket asserts an `<svg class="conv-line">` with exactly 4 `<polyline>` (one per decision series)'
The number 4 in the AC conflates round count with decision-series count; the plan lists up to 4 series (BLOCKING, advisory, dropped, indeterminate) but a concrete fixture may have fewer.
findings.finding: The convergence-viz AC for a ≥4-round ticket asserts 'exactly 4 `<polyline>`' but the plan defines polyline count as 'one `<polyline>` per decision series' (BLOCKING, advisory, dropped, indeterminate), not per round. A 4-round ticket whose findings only hit two decision series (e.g., BLOCKING and advisory) would produce 2 polylines, making the assertion falsely fail. Conversely, a 5-round ticket with 4 decision series also satisfies 'exactly 4' — the count is not tied to rounds at all.
findings.location: AC for convergence visualization: 'a 4-round ticket asserts an `<svg class="conv-line">` with exactly 4 `<polyline>` (one per decision series) and NO `.conv-bars`'
scenarios: An E2E fixture for a 4-round ticket where every round only produces BLOCKING and advisory findings emits 2 polylines; the oracle's 'exactly 4' assertion fails even though the implementation is correct.
A fixture author adds findings of all 4 decision types to satisfy the '4 polyline' count — but then the fixture is testing cardinality of decision types, not round count.
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the oracle as: 'a 4-round ticket whose fixture data contains exactly K distinct decision series asserts an `<svg class="conv-line">` with exactly K `<polyline>` elements and NO `.conv-bars`; and asserts the visually-hidden `<table>` has exactly 4 data rows (one per round).' This separates the round-count assertion (table rows) from the series-count assertion (polylines).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the completion panel renders when only a FAIL sidecar exists (no `criteria[]`): either fall back to the FAIL record's `findings[]` to show failing criteria rows, or show an explicit 'per-criterion detail unavailable (FAIL record only)' message, so the panel is never silently empty for a failed completion.
evidence: Plan states: 'These per-criterion rows read STORY C's `criteria[]` field (`{criterion, met, citation, kind}`)' and 'a field the pre-enrichment completion verdict does NOT carry (its `findings` are failures-only, with no `met` or `kind`)'
completion_sidecar.py FAIL branch (line 222–231): FAIL schema record contains only `findings`, `remediation`, `certifiable` — no `criteria[]`
completion_sidecar.py PASS branch (line 211–221): PASS schema record carries `criteria[]`
read.py _completion_sidecar_record(): returns latest_pass_record first, falls back to latest_fail_verdict — meaning a ticket with only a FAIL record yields a sidecar with no `criteria[]`
ABSENCE: the plan does not specify what the completion panel renders when `criteria[]` is absent from the sidecar (FAIL-only ticket)
findings.finding: The completion panel's per-criterion row mechanism breaks silently when only a FAIL completion sidecar exists. The plan states the panel reads story C's `criteria[]` field (met/not-met/kind/citation), but the `completion_verifier_fail_v1` schema (the FAIL sidecar) does NOT carry `criteria[]` — only `findings[]` (failures only). The `audit_trail` read layer returns the PASS sidecar when it exists (which carries `criteria[]`), but when only a FAIL record exists, `criteria[]` is absent. The template receives no per-criterion rows to render for a ticket that has failed completion but never passed.
findings.location: What section — Completion section; src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py build_payload (FAIL branch, lines 222–231)
scenarios: A ticket has been run through completion verification and failed (FAIL sidecar exists, no PASS sidecar). The completion panel has no `criteria[]` to display per-criterion rows. The page either shows an empty panel body (misleading — user can't see what failed) or throws a template error.
A ticket whose completion PASS sidecar was pruned (retention bound = 10) while a FAIL sidecar still exists — the panel loses its criteria[] rows.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fallback rendering path: when `completion.sidecar.schema == 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'` (or `criteria[]` is absent), display the failure findings from `findings[]` in the criterion rows instead, with a 'not-met' glyph and a note that positive criteria detail requires a PASS record. Update the relevant AC oracle to cover this case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the convergence viz series count: either state explicitly that 'overflow' findings are merged into the 'advisory' series for the line graph (making 4 series: blocking / advisory+overflow / dropped / indeterminate), or change the oracle to 'at most 5 polylines' and name all 5 series; update the AC6 oracle to match.
evidence: AC6 oracle: 'a 4-round ticket asserts an `<svg class="conv-line">` with exactly 4 `<polyline>` (one per decision series)'
Plan body: 'one `<polyline>` per decision series (BLOCKING prominent, advisory/dropped/indeterminate muted)'
plan_review/sidecar.py build_payload (line 421–427): buckets = blocking + advisory + overflow + indeterminate + dropped — 5 distinct series in the data model
Plan body mentions 'advisory/dropped/indeterminate' as muted series — 3 muted + 1 blocking = 4, implying 'overflow' is either merged with 'advisory' or silently dropped, but this is never stated
findings.finding: The convergence visualization AC oracle for ≥4-round tickets asserts 'exactly 4 `<polyline>` (one per decision series)', but the plan-review sidecar buckets findings into up to 5 series (blocking / advisory / overflow / indeterminate / dropped), making '4 polylines' an internally inconsistent oracle that will either be wrong for typical data or require the implementer to silently merge or drop a series.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC6; What section — convergence visualization (≥4 rounds, `<svg class="conv-line">`)
scenarios: Implementer renders 5 polylines (one per actual bucket) — AC oracle fails ('exactly 4').
Implementer merges 'overflow' into 'advisory' for the visualization — correct behavior but undocumented, making it a latent surprise when overflow findings are non-zero.
A review round has overflow findings; the line graph either misrepresents them or the implementer must invent a merging rule the plan doesn't specify.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'For the line graph, the `overflow` series is merged into `advisory` (both are non-blocking surfaced findings); the four rendered series are: BLOCKING / ADVISORY (includes overflow) / DROPPED / INDETERMINATE.' Update the AC6 oracle to 'exactly 4 `<polyline>` representing blocking, advisory+overflow, dropped, and indeterminate series.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify how per-round timestamps reach the server: either extend `all_review_results()` / `audit_trail` to surface the event-envelope `timestamp` per sidecar round, or document the server-side mechanism (filename parse, extra read), since the current read layer discards it.
evidence: Plan says: 'Trust signals in each round header (quiet caption): gate/model version, timestamp, and a distinct "certified" badge near the verdict when the record is signed.'
`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` `all_review_results()` lines 210-215: appends `event.get('data')` — the outer envelope (including `timestamp`) is discarded.
`src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py` line 362: outer event structure is `{timestamp, uuid, event_type, env_id, author, data}` — timestamp is envelope-level, not payload-level.
`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` `build_payload()` lines 428-454: no `timestamp` field in the returned dict.
`src/rebar/audit/read.py` `AuditTrail` TypedDict: `plan_reviews: list[dict]` — no timestamp surfaced per round.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'timestamp' as an available trust signal in each plan-review round header, but the `all_review_results()` function (consumed by `audit_trail`) extracts only `event['data']` from each sidecar file, discarding the outer event envelope's `timestamp` field. The sidecar payload (`plan_review_result_v2`) has no timestamp field of its own. The `AuditTrail` TypedDict exposes no timestamp per round. The server cannot display per-round timestamps as claimed without additional work not described in the plan.
findings.location: What / Trust signals subsection; also Scope `src/rebar/audit/server.py`
scenarios: Server renders the round header trust-signal caption but has no timestamp data — silently omits it or crashes on a missing key.
Implementer is forced to re-derive the timestamp from a filename parse or a separate read of the event envelope, neither of which is specified by the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: In `all_review_results()`, return `{'ts': <filename_prefix_ns>, **payload}` (or an equivalent wrapper dict) so `audit_trail`'s `plan_reviews` list includes the nanosecond timestamp from the filename, which the server can format for display.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the 'certified' badge maps to the read layer: either add a plan-review attestation fetch to `audit_trail` (analogous to `_completion_attestation`), or document that the badge is ticket-level and specify which round(s) it applies to.
evidence: Plan says: 'a distinct "certified" badge near the verdict when the record is signed.'
`src/rebar/audit/read.py` `AuditTrail` TypedDict: `plan_reviews: list[dict]` carries no signing/certification status field.
`src/rebar/audit/read.py` `_completion_attestation()`: only completion-verifier attestation is fetched — no equivalent for plan-review.
`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` `build_payload()` lines 428-454: sidecar payload has no `signed` or `certified` field.
`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py`: plan-review attestation is ticket-level (one SIGNATURE event), not per-round.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a distinct certified badge near the verdict when the record is signed' in each plan-review round header. Plan-review signing produces a ticket-level attestation (`SIGNATURE` event via `attest.py`), not a per-round field in the sidecar. The `AuditTrail` read layer (`read.py`) exposes no plan-review attestation status — only the completion attestation is read (`_completion_attestation`). Displaying a per-round certified badge would require an additional `verify_signature(ticket_id, kind=_MANIFEST_PREFIX)` call the plan never specifies.
findings.location: What / Trust signals subsection
scenarios: Server attempts to show a certified badge but has no source for signing status in the read layer — either silently omits it or calls an unspecified API.
The badge is displayed per-round but actually reflects only the current ticket-level attestation, not the specific round (rounds are not individually signed).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `timestamp` (from the outer event envelope or filename prefix) to the per-round payloads surfaced by the read layer, OR explicitly note in Scope how the server route obtains it — the current read layer (`all_review_results` for both plan-review and code-review sidecars) strips the outer envelope and the inner payload carries no `timestamp` field.
evidence: Plan: 'Trust signals in each round header (quiet caption): gate/model version, timestamp, and a distinct
certified" badge near the verdict when the record is signed.'"
`all_review_results` (both plan and code review): `payload = event.get('data')` — the outer envelope's `timestamp` key is discarded.
`build_payload` result for plan review (sidecar.py:428–454): no `timestamp` key in the returned dict.
The outer event envelope (assembled by `append_event` in `_seam.py:360`) carries `timestamp`, but it is not forwarded into the payload stored at `event['data']`.
ABSENCE: No edit to `audit/read.py` or either sidecar's `all_review_results` is listed in Scope.
findings.finding: The plan promises a `timestamp` trust signal in each round header ('gate/model version, timestamp'), but both `plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results` and `code_review.sidecar.all_review_results` return only the inner `event['data']` payload dict — which does NOT include `timestamp`. The outer event envelope (stripped by both readers) carries the timestamp; the inner payload does not. The scope lists only `server.py` and templates as edit targets, with no mention of extending the read layer to surface per-round timestamps.
findings.location: Scope section (`src/rebar/audit/server.py`); plan 'Trust signals' bullet; `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` lines 205–215 (`all_review_results`); `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` lines 310–319 (`all_review_results`)
scenarios: The server renders the round header but has no timestamp to show — either the field is silently absent (a blank caption) or the template raises a KeyError/AttributeError.

An implementer must either (a) modify `audit/read.py` to include the outer envelope timestamp when collecting round payloads, or (b) parse the sidecar filename prefix — neither path is specified in scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `audit/read.py` (or both sidecar `all_review_results` helpers) to inject the outer envelope `timestamp` into each returned payload dict (e.g. `payload['_ts'] = event.get('timestamp')`), then add `audit/read.py` to the Scope edit list and update the AC trust-signals oracle to assert the timestamp field is present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the round-selection mechanism explicitly: either (a) URL query parameter (e.g., `?round=n`) causing a full server-rendered page reload (the only no-JS interactive selection path), or (b) all rounds rendered simultaneously with the stepper being a scroll-anchor, and update the AC7 oracle accordingly.
evidence: Plan states: 'No JS' and 'Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function of the read-layer data'
Plan states: '◂ round n of N ▸ stepper (newest default)', 'selecting an OLDER round renders that round's distinct content (findings + convergence)'
AC7 oracle: 'requesting the older round renders its (distinct-from-latest) findings' — implies a navigation action changes the rendered content
ABSENCE: the plan does not name the URL parameter, form method, or any other concrete no-JS round-selection mechanism
findings.finding: The `◂ round n of N ▸` round-selection stepper mechanism is unspecified: the plan says 'no JS; server-rendered Jinja2' but also says 'selecting an OLDER round renders that round's distinct content'. These two constraints can only be reconciled via URL query parameter navigation (e.g., `?round=2` → full page reload), but the plan never names this mechanism. Without a specified mechanism, an implementer might render all rounds simultaneously (no selection needed), use a `<form>` POST, or misread the constraint and introduce JS.
findings.location: What section — Plan-review and code-review sections (round navigation); Acceptance Criteria AC7
scenarios: Implementer renders all rounds stacked on the page (always showing all N rounds at once) — technically satisfies 'no JS' and 'server-rendered' but doesn't satisfy 'selecting an older round renders that round's distinct content'.

Implementer uses a <form method='GET'> with a round selector — valid no-JS, but then the sticky gate-strip and heading anchor behavior changes with each form submission, which may conflict with the UX intent.
AC7 oracle's 'requesting the older round' is ambiguous: a URL param (?round=1) is the obvious interpretation but is never stated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'The `◂ round n of N ▸` stepper is implemented as an `<a href="?round=n">` link (GET query parameter); the route reads `?round=` and passes the selected sidecar index to the template. The default (no `?round=` param) renders the newest round.' Update AC7 oracle to assert the `?round=1` URL renders the older round.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default round-navigation mechanism in the plan (e.g., 'round stepper emits `?round=N` query-param GET links; server reads `request.args.get("round", latest)` and renders the selected round') so the executor has a concrete contract to implement and test against.
evidence: 'no JS; keyboard + screen-reader support for free' (What section, first bullet)
'Round navigation as a `◂ round n of N ▸` stepper (newest default), in a sticky section header.' (plan-review and code-review sections bullet)
'selecting an OLDER round renders that round's distinct content' (AC line 7) — mechanism unspecified
'Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function of the read-layer data' (Approach rationale) — implies no JS, so round switching must be a GET, but no query-param or form mechanism is named
findings.finding: The round stepper (`◂ round n of N ▸`) must render different round content on a server-rendered, no-JS page, but the plan never specifies the navigation mechanism (e.g., query-param GET link `?round=N`, a `<form>` POST, or anchor-only). Without a stated default, an executing agent must invent the mechanism — a real design choice the plan defers to the executor.
findings.location: What section — 'Round navigation as a `◂ round n of N ▸` stepper' + AC for round selector (AC line 7)
scenarios: An agent implementing the stepper must arbitrarily choose between `?round=N` query-param links, a `<form method=GET>` with a select element, or next/prev anchor-text links — picking the wrong one could break the E2E oracle ('requesting the older round') or the no-JS constraint.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Scope or What section: 'The round stepper renders as `<a href="?round=N">` links; the route reads `round` from the query-string (default: latest) and passes the selected round's data to the template.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the threshold meter fallback for findings lacking a persisted `block_threshold` (v1 sidecars): e.g. use `DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD` (0.95) as the tick, suppress the meter, or show a 'legacy — threshold unknown' label.
evidence: Plan says: 'the server reads it directly off the finding record — it does NOT do a runtime `criteria_routing` lookup.'
`tests/interfaces/store/test_sidecar_reader.py` line 190: `assert 'block_threshold' not in got_v1['findings'][0]` — v1 records confirmed to lack this field.
`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` `all_review_results()` lines 210-214: accepts both `plan_review_result_v1` and `plan_review_result_v2` schemas.
AC for threshold meter: 'seeding two findings with KNOWN, DIFFERENT per-finding `block_threshold` values' — implies always-present, but v1 findings lack it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the server reads [block_threshold] directly off the finding record' and the threshold meter AC requires each finding's tick position to reflect its own persisted threshold. However, v1 sidecar records (`plan_review_result_v1`) do not carry `block_threshold` on findings — confirmed by test `test_sidecar_reader.py:190` (`assert 'block_threshold' not in got_v1['findings'][0]`). `all_review_results()` returns both v1 and v2 payloads. The plan never specifies what the threshold meter should render when `block_threshold` is absent (older rounds or v1 history).
findings.location: What / Plan-review and code-review sections / threshold display; AC for threshold meter
scenarios: A ticket with retained v1-schema sidecar rounds is opened in the audit UI; the threshold meter has null/undefined `block_threshold` per finding and the template crashes or renders a broken bar.
The round selector shows a v1 round; the tick mark is absent with no documented fallback (e.g. show DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD=0.95, or hide the meter).
findings.suggested_fix: In the template, treat a missing `block_threshold` as `DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD = 0.95` (matching the historical default the criterion would have been judged against) and note this in the tick's tooltip.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify v1 sidecar backward-compatibility rendering: when a finding lacks `block_threshold`, render the meter without a tick mark (or with a 'threshold unavailable' label); when `evidence`/`scenarios` are absent, omit those subsections from the provenance block rather than rendering empty headings.
evidence: Plan states: 'The threshold meter reads each finding's persisted `block_threshold`/`priority`/`decision`'
Plan states: 'Finding (pass-1 text, mapped criteria, location, evidence, scenarios, impact, suggested fix)'
test_sidecar_reader.py line 189–190: 'assert "evidence" not in got_v1["findings"][0]' and 'assert "block_threshold" not in got_v1["findings"][0]' — v1 findings explicitly lack these fields
ABSENCE: no template fallback behavior specified for v1 records (e.g., 'hide meter tick', 'omit evidence section', 'show unavailable placeholder')
findings.finding: The threshold meter and four-pass provenance display have no specified fallback for `plan_review_result_v1` sidecar records, which lack `block_threshold`, `evidence`, and `scenarios` fields (confirmed by the codebase: v1 records never carry these fields). The plan states the meter 'reads each finding's persisted `block_threshold`/`priority`/`decision`' and provenance displays 'pass-1 evidence', but does not specify what the template renders when these fields are absent on a v1 finding.
findings.location: What section — threshold meter; four-pass provenance; tests/interfaces/store/test_sidecar_reader.py lines 171–190
scenarios: A ticket has a retained v1 plan-review sidecar (any review run before story 4e19 shipped). The threshold meter has no block_threshold to place the tick; the template either renders a broken meter (tick at None/0) or throws a Jinja2 AttributeError.
The four-pass provenance section tries to render evidence[] and scenarios[] sections that are absent on v1 findings — template either shows empty sections (confusing) or errors.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/templates section: 'Templates must guard all v2-only fields (`block_threshold`, `evidence`, `scenarios`) with `{% if finding.block_threshold is not none %}` / `{% if finding.evidence %}` guards; v1 findings render the meter bar (priority fill) without a tick, and the provenance block omits the evidence/scenarios subsections with no visible gap.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a contrast obligation to the Visual System description (e.g. 'all text and UI component colors meet WCAG 1.4.3 AA: 4.5:1 for body/chip text, 3:1 for graphical meter components') and add an observable done-definition to AC 10 (or a new AC) asserting that verdict/decision chip and meter fill colors meet the stated ratios.
evidence: 'a strict 3-level type scale; saturated color reserved for verdict + decision chips only (everything else neutral grey)'
WCAG 1.4.10 is explicitly cited; WCAG 1.4.3 is absent from the plan entirely.
No AC asserts any contrast ratio or references a design-token constraint that would guarantee compliance.
findings.finding: The plan introduces colored verdict/decision chips, a threshold meter bar with fill color, and a 3-level type scale, but never commits to any contrast ratio target (WCAG 1.4.3: 4.5:1 normal text, 3:1 large/UI components). 'Saturated color reserved for verdict + decision chips' signals deliberate use of color without a corresponding contrast obligation, leaving a WCAG 2.1 AA gap unaddressed in both the plan body and all ACs.
findings.location: What section ('Visual system' bullet); Approach Rationale ('Server-rendered Jinja2 … single-column stacking survives narrow width and 200% zoom (WCAG 1.4.10)'); no AC covers contrast.
scenarios: Saturated chip colors chosen for aesthetics fail 4.5:1 against their backgrounds; the plan provides no guardrail.
A reviewer approves the implementation against ACs 1–11 with no contrast check, shipping a WCAG AA failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 10 to include: 'and that the verdict chip foreground/background color pair meets WCAG 1.4.3 AA contrast (≥4.5:1 for normal-size text).' In the Visual System bullet, add: 'all colors meet WCAG 1.4.3 AA minimum contrast.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a focus-visibility constraint to the Scope or Visual System section (e.g. 'inline CSS MUST NOT suppress browser focus outlines on interactive elements; if custom focus styles are applied they must remain visible') and extend AC 10's oracle to assert that `<summary>` and anchor elements retain a visible focus indicator (e.g. assert absence of `outline: none` or presence of a `:focus-visible` rule in the rendered CSS).
evidence: Scope: 'inline CSS; the convergence visualization + threshold meter as inline SVG/CSS (no external JS/asset)'
AC 10 oracle: 'asserting `<details>` elements and the heading structure are present in the rendered HTML' — no assertion on focus ring visibility.
WCAG 2.4.7 (Focus Visible, AA) not mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan applies inline CSS to templates but never commits to preserving visible focus indicators (WCAG 2.4.7 AA: any keyboard-focusable component has a visible focus indicator). Native `<details>/<summary>` and `<a>` elements carry browser-default focus rings, but inline CSS overrides (e.g. `outline: none` applied to chips, anchors, or summary elements for aesthetics) can silently remove them. No AC or scope statement prohibits this or requires focus visibility.
findings.location: Scope ('inline CSS'); What ('each collapsible a native `<details>`'); AC 10 (tests `<details>` presence and heading structure but not focus visibility)
scenarios: An inline CSS rule suppresses the default `outline` on `<summary>` elements or anchor chips for visual polish; keyboard users lose the focus indicator with no failing test to catch it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit non-happy-path spec (and a matching AC oracle) for: (a) ticket ID not found → user-facing 404/error panel, not a stack trace; (b) read-layer unavailability → a graceful error state; (c) invalid round parameter in the round selector → falls back to the latest round.
evidence: "Empty/partial states: each absent gate shows a quiet, text-only 'not yet run' panel, never hidden." — covers absent gate data but not absent ticket.
No AC or prose addresses HTTP 404, invalid ticket ID, or read-layer failure responses.
Route definition: 'add the /ticket/<id> route consuming the story-E read layer' — no error-handling contract stated.
findings.finding: The plan covers empty/partial gate data ('not yet run' panels) but never specifies what the page does when the ticket ID is not found in the read layer (a 404 or equivalent error state) or when the read layer itself returns an error — leaving the highest-stakes error path (dead link, stale bookmark, deleted ticket) unspecified.
findings.location: Empty/partial states bullet + Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
scenarios: A user bookmarks /ticket/old-id after the ticket is archived or the ID is mistyped — the server has no specified behavior and may emit a 500 or blank page.
A CI system generates a report link before the read-layer record is committed — the page loads mid-write with a partial or absent record.
The read layer is temporarily unavailable; the server has no specified fallback or user-facing error message.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one AC: 'Requesting /ticket/<nonexistent-id> returns a rendered error page (not a 500) with a clear message. Requesting an invalid round parameter defaults silently to the latest round. Oracle: E2E tests asserting HTTP 404 + error-panel HTML for a missing ticket, and latest-round rendering for an out-of-range round query param.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and oracle E2E test) specifying the server's behavior when `/ticket/<id>` is called with a non-existent ticket ID — e.g., renders a named 'ticket not found' panel and returns HTTP 404 — so the failure path is covered by the test suite.
evidence: AC9 reads: 'A ticket with no code review / no completion verdict shows an explicit "not yet run" panel, not a blank or error. Oracle: an E2E test on a plan-review-only ticket.' — this seeds a *valid* ticket with partial data, not an absent ticket record.
No AC specifies HTTP status code or rendered response when the ID is not found in the read layer.
The plan states 'Empty/partial states: each absent gate shows a quiet, text-only "not yet run" panel' — absent gate ≠ absent ticket; the 404 path is undescribed.
findings.finding: The `/ticket/<id>` route has no specified behavior or acceptance-criterion test for the case where the ticket ID does not exist in the read layer (e.g., a stale URL, typo, or a ticket deleted between runs). AC9 only covers a *valid* ticket with missing gate results, not a missing ticket record itself. For a new user-facing route this is a missing failure path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC9 ('A ticket with no code review / no completion verdict shows an explicit "not yet run" panel'); Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py` route `/ticket/<id>`
scenarios: An auditor follows a stale link to a ticket ID that no longer exists (or was never indexed) — the server returns an unspecified response (possibly a 500 or empty page) and no test catches the regression.
A deployment bug in the read layer omits a ticket; the audit page silently errors rather than surfacing a clear 'not found' message.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'Requesting `/ticket/<id>` for a ticket ID that does not exist in the read layer returns HTTP 404 and renders a named "ticket not found" panel (not a blank page or 500). Oracle: an E2E test seeding the read layer with no record for a known ID, asserting HTTP 404 and the presence of the not-found panel in the response body.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit error-handling specification to the `/ticket/<id>` route: define behavior for unknown ticket IDs (404 with a clear message), read-layer exceptions (500 with a safe error page), and any partial-record states that fall outside the 'not yet run' panel logic.
evidence: ABSENCE: no error_handling, try/except, HTTP status, or graceful-degradation specification anywhere in the plan
'each absent gate shows a quiet, text-only "not yet run" panel, never hidden' — covers missing gate data only, not route-level errors
AC list has no oracle for 404/500 behavior
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling for the `/ticket/<id>` route when the read layer fails (e.g., unknown ticket ID returning 404 vs 500, read-layer exception, partial/corrupt record). The only 'absent data' handling described is the UI-level 'not yet run' panel, which addresses absent gate data but not server-side I/O failures.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py`; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers error paths)
scenarios: A caller requests a ticket ID that does not exist in the read layer — the route either throws an unhandled exception (500) or returns a blank page with no user guidance.
The read layer's backing store is temporarily unavailable; the route propagates a raw exception rather than a safe error page.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section for `server.py`, add: 'The route returns a 404 page (with a human-readable message) for unknown ticket IDs and a 500 page (with a safe error message, no raw traceback) for read-layer exceptions; both are tested by dedicated E2E oracle cases.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify at minimum a structured request/error log for the `/ticket/<id>` route (e.g., log ticket ID, HTTP status, and read-layer latency per request) so failures are observable without code changes.
evidence: ABSENCE: no mention of logging, structured error logs, metrics (request count, error rate, latency), or tracing in any section of the plan
T5b rubric: 'New failure points instrumented with a metric/log/trace/alert'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new HTTP route and a read-layer dependency but specifies no observability (no logging, metrics, or tracing) for the new failure point, leaving operators with no signal when the route errors or the read layer is slow/unavailable.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py`; Compatibility & rollback section
scenarios: The read layer silently fails or becomes slow; without a log or metric, operators have no visibility into degraded audit-page behavior.
A spike in 500 errors on the audit route goes undetected because no error-rate alert or access log is specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the round-navigation control description: if it is a prev/next stepper showing '◂ n of N ▸', update AC 7's oracle to assert the rendered label (e.g. '◂ round 2 of 3 ▸') rather than 'lists … rounds newest-first'; if it is an enumerated round picker/list, update the body description to match and specify what 'listing' looks like (e.g. a `<select>` or inline link list).
evidence: Body: 'Round navigation as a `◂ round n of N ▸` stepper (newest default), in a sticky section header.'

AC 7: 'the `◂ round n of N ▸` selector lists the retained plan- and code-review rounds newest-first, defaults to the latest … asserting the selector shows N, the default is the latest, and requesting the older round renders its (distinct-from-latest) findings.'
'lists … rounds' and 'shows N' implies enumerating all rounds, which a pure prev/next stepper does not do.
findings.finding: The plan body describes round navigation as a '◂ round n of N ▸ stepper' (implying prev/next buttons that traverse one round at a time) while AC 7 describes 'the selector lists the retained plan- and code-review rounds newest-first' and verifies 'the selector shows N' — a listing/enumeration affordance more consistent with a dropdown or explicit round list. These are different UI patterns: a stepper does not 'list' rounds and does not trivially 'show N'; a round-list selector does not naturally present as '◂ n of N ▸'. An implementer would not know which to build.
findings.location: What section ('Round navigation as a `◂ round n of N ▸` stepper') vs AC 7 ('the `◂ round n of N ▸` selector lists the retained … rounds newest-first … asserting the selector shows N')
scenarios: Implementer builds a prev/next stepper (matching body description) but AC 7 assertion 'selector shows N' fails because the stepper shows only ◂/▸ buttons, not a count of N enumerated rounds.
Implementer builds a dropdown listing all rounds but the '◂ n of N ▸' label is never rendered, failing a different read of AC 7.
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt the stepper framing throughout: body stays as-is; AC 7 oracle changes to 'asserting the rendered stepper label shows the correct n-of-N text for the latest round, and that navigating to the prior round (via the ◂ link) renders that round's distinct findings' — which is server-renderable with plain `<a>` href links and no JS.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or `CLAUDE.md` / design-doc section capturing the three core architectural decisions (finding-centric IA, neutral threshold meter, fixed canonical section order + sticky strip) so future maintainers have a durable, discoverable rationale beyond the story text.
evidence: Approach rationale section explains the design choices inline in the story
"src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html" introduces a new UI architecture pattern
No mention of ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions (finding-centric IA over pass-as-navigation, neutral threshold meter design, fixed canonical section order, native `<details>` collapsibles as the no-JS pattern) whose rationale is captured only in the story document itself — no ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update is mentioned. Future maintainers extending or refactoring the audit surface will lack a durable, discoverable record of why these choices were made.
findings.location: Approach rationale section; Scope section
scenarios: A future maintainer adding a new gate type doesn't understand why sections are fixed-order vs. reordered by severity, violates spatial-memory invariant
A developer refactoring the threshold meter doesn't know why the neutral tick design was chosen over a signed delta, reintroduces the green=cleared-bar contradiction
The finding-centric vs. pass-centric IA decision gets relitigated without the HCD critique context
findings.suggested_fix: Create or append to `docs/adr/audit-page-design.md` (or the project's existing ADR location) with: (1) finding-centric IA rationale (auditors reason in findings, not passes; HCD critique reference), (2) neutral threshold meter rationale (avoids green=cleared-bar contradiction, color inherits decision), (3) fixed canonical section order + sticky strip rationale (spatial memory + attention routing). Cross-reference the story in the ADR header.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the ≤3/≥4 convergence visualization branch threshold into a named constant or template variable (e.g., `CONV_BAR_MAX_ROUNDS = 3`) passed from `server.py` so it can be tuned without a template edit.
evidence: "for ≤3 rounds, a `<div class="conv-bars">`... for ≥4 rounds, an `<svg class="conv-line">`"
"The two forms are mutually exclusive (exactly one is emitted)"
No mention of this threshold being configurable or derived from a config value
findings.finding: The convergence visualization branch threshold (≤3 rounds → bar form; ≥4 rounds → line form) is a hardcoded value in template logic with no configuration point. If UX research or usage data suggests this boundary should shift (e.g., ≤2 or ≤5), it requires a template code change rather than a configuration update.
findings.location: What section — convergence visualization spec; Scope: `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html`
scenarios: Post-launch UX feedback shows 3-round bar charts are too cluttered or 4-round line charts are too sparse; adjusting the boundary requires a template code change and re-deployment rather than a config tweak
findings.suggested_fix: In `server.py`, define `CONV_BAR_MAX_ROUNDS = 3` and pass it into the template context; the template uses `{% if round_count <= conv_bar_max %}` rather than a hardcoded literal. This keeps the heuristic visible and changeable in one place.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare `jinja2` as an explicit dependency: either add it to `[project.dependencies]` (if the audit server is always-on) or create an `[audit]` extra in `pyproject.toml` and register it in `_optional.py` EXTRAS so the server's startup path can call `require_extra('audit')` and emit a clear install hint.
evidence: Search for 'jinja2' across all files returns zero hits in src/rebar
pyproject.toml has no 'jinja2' entry in [project.dependencies] or any [project.optional-dependencies] extra
_optional.py EXTRAS registry lists agents/eval/tracing/grounding — no 'audit' extra with a jinja2 probe

Existing HTML-serving code at src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor.py build_host_html (line ~296–402) uses a raw f-string with _js_embed — the codebase's established pattern for serving HTML from Python without a template engine
jinja2 resolves as third-party in the installed environment only (transitive, not a direct declared dep)
findings.finding: The plan mandates 'server-rendered Jinja2' but jinja2 is not declared as a direct dependency or in any pyproject.toml extra, and it is entirely absent from src/rebar (zero existing usage, not registered in _optional.py). The codebase's established HTML-serving pattern (workflow editor.py `build_host_html`) uses stdlib f-strings + `_js_embed` with no template engine. Introducing Jinja2 adds a new undeclared runtime dependency that will only be incidentally present in the installed environment via transitives.
findings.location: Scope section: 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)'; Approach rationale: 'Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function of the read-layer data'
scenarios: A production install of 'nava-rebar[agents]' (or any existing extra) may not have jinja2 on the path if the transitive that currently pulls it is updated or removed.
The audit server fails to import jinja2 at startup with an opaque ModuleNotFoundError rather than the project's standard OptionalDependencyError pointing to the correct pip install command.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `jinja2>=3` to a new `[project.optional-dependencies] audit = ["jinja2>=3"]` entry in `pyproject.toml` and add `"audit": ("jinja2", "the audit UI server")` to `_optional.py`'s EXTRAS map; have `server.py` call `require_extra("audit")` before importing jinja2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare `jinja2` (or `"fastapi[jinja2]"`) in the `pyproject.toml` extra that the audit server story will use, and name that extra explicitly in the Scope/Compatibility section.
evidence: pyproject.toml `reviewbot` extra: `["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` — jinja2 absent
pyproject.toml has zero occurrences of 'jinja2' in any extra or dependency list
fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates resolves EXISTS in the dev venv, confirming it works when jinja2 is installed — but the venv state is not the declared pyproject.toml state

Prior plan-review cache hit: `.rebar/cache/plan-review/ff6f-b655-4058-4308/c31a370f5d383f0caf96.json` flagged 'jinja2 is not declared as a direct dependency or in any pyproject.toml extra' on the parent server-scaffolding story
findings.finding: The plan uses `fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` (Jinja2 server-rendered templates) but `jinja2` is not declared as a dependency anywhere in `pyproject.toml` — not in `[project.dependencies]` nor in the `reviewbot` extra (which lists only `fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, `boto3`). FastAPI intentionally does not bundle Jinja2; it is an optional dep requiring either `pip install 'fastapi[jinja2]'` or an explicit `jinja2` entry. The plan names no extra that will receive this dep, nor does it mention adding it.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html` (Jinja2); Approach rationale: 'Server-rendered Jinja2 keeps the page a pure function'
scenarios: A CI environment (or user) installs `nava-rebar[reviewbot]` or the new audit extra and runs `rebar audit serve` — the `Jinja2Templates` instantiation raises `ImportError: 'jinja2' package is not installed` at startup, even though FastAPI itself is present.
The server-scaffolding story ships its own extra without jinja2; this story adds templates but no new dep — both are silently broken in a clean install.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"jinja2>=3"` to whichever extra the server-scaffolding story creates for the audit UI (e.g. `audit = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2>=3"]`), and reference that extra by name in this plan's Scope section.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
